title: Deploying Your First ML Model: A Practical Checklist
author: Rohit Ghumare
excerpt: Moving from a Jupyter notebook to a production system is where most ML projects die. Here's a checklist to keep yours alive.
content:
The Notebook Graveyard

Walk into any data science team and you'll find dozens of beautiful notebooks: clean experiments, beautiful charts, and a model that scored 0.94 AUC on the validation set. Then ask: where is it running in production? Silence.

The gap between experiment and production is not a technical gap — it's a discipline gap. This checklist is designed to bridge it.

Pre-Deployment

  • Version your data and code. Use DVC or a similar tool. If you can't reproduce the training run, you can't debug it.
  • Define success metrics in production. Offline metrics like AUC are proxies. Track real business outcomes: conversion rate, support ticket volume, error rate.
  • Build a rollback plan. How do you revert to the previous model in under five minutes?
  • Document the model card. What data was used? What are the known failure modes? What groups might the model treat unfairly?

Infrastructure

  • Containerize the model. Docker is the default. Your inference environment should match training exactly.
  • Expose a standard API. REST with JSON is fine for most cases. Consider gRPC for high-throughput internal services.
  • Add health checks. Kubernetes or any orchestrator needs to know when your service is unhealthy.
  • Log predictions and inputs. Not for surveillance — for debugging. When a prediction goes wrong, you need to reconstruct what happened.

Monitoring

  • Track prediction latency and throughput. Set alerts for P99 latency spikes.
  • Monitor input drift. If the distribution of incoming features shifts, your model's assumptions may no longer hold.
  • Monitor output drift. Sudden changes in prediction distributions can signal upstream data issues or model degradation.
  • Set up shadow mode. Run the new model alongside the old one without serving its predictions. Compare outcomes before going live.

The Human Loop

Even the best models need escape hatches. Build interfaces for:

  • Overriding predictions when domain experts disagree.
  • Flagging edge cases that the model has never seen.
  • Reporting errors so feedback flows back into retraining.

Conclusion

Deployment is not the finish line — it's the starting line of a different race. The models that survive are the ones wrapped in systems that observe, adapt, and degrade gracefully. Use this checklist, adapt it to your context, and ship something that lasts.
